title: Demand & Elasticity
simple_html:
Demand describes how much of a product buyers will purchase at each price. Elasticity measures how sensitive that quantity is to a change in price (or income, or the price of another good). Together they tell a manager how customers will react to a pricing move.

The law of demand

All else equal, when price rises, quantity demanded falls; when price falls, quantity rises. That is why demand curves slope downward.

Price elasticity of demand

Price elasticity (Ed) = percentage change in quantity demanded divided by percentage change in price. Because the two move in opposite directions, the number is negative, but we usually talk about its size:

  • Elastic (|Ed| > 1): quantity reacts strongly. Cutting price raises total revenue.
  • Inelastic (|Ed| < 1): quantity barely reacts. Raising price raises total revenue.
  • Unit elastic (|Ed| = 1): revenue is unchanged.

What makes demand elastic?

More substitutes, the good being a luxury, a bigger share of the budget, and more time to adjust all make demand more elastic. Necessities with few substitutes (insulin, salt) are inelastic.

Other elasticities

Income elasticity shows how demand changes with income (positive for normal goods, negative for inferior goods). Cross-price elasticity shows how the demand for one good changes when another's price changes (positive = substitutes, negative = complements).

Key takeaways

  • Demand slopes down (law of demand).
  • Elastic → cut price to grow revenue; inelastic → raise price.
  • Income elasticity classifies normal vs inferior goods.
  • Cross elasticity reveals substitutes vs complements.
intermediate_html:
Demand analysis answers two managerial questions: how many units will sell at a given price, and how will buyers respond to changes in price, income, or competitors' prices? Elasticity is the quantitative answer, and it directly drives pricing and revenue decisions.

The demand function

Quantity demanded depends on the good's own price, consumer income, prices of related goods, tastes, and expectations: Qd = f(P, I, Pr, T, ...). A change in own price moves you along a demand curve (a change in quantity demanded); a change in any other determinant shifts the whole curve (a change in demand). Confusing the two is a classic error.

Price elasticity of demand

Ed = (%ΔQ) / (%ΔP). Two formulas matter. The point elasticity uses a single point: Ed = (dQ/dP)(P/Q). The arc (midpoint) elasticity uses averages to avoid the asymmetry between two points:

Ed = [(Q2 - Q1)/((Q1+Q2)/2)] / [(P2 - P1)/((P1+P2)/2)]

The midpoint method gives the same answer whether price rises or falls, which is why it is preferred for discrete changes.

Elasticity and total revenue

The link to revenue is the most exam-relevant result. Total revenue TR = P x Q. Whether a price cut raises or lowers TR depends on elasticity:

Demand|Ed|Price upPrice down
Elastic> 1TR fallsTR rises
Unit elastic= 1TR unchangedTR unchanged
Inelastic< 1TR risesTR falls

On a straight-line demand curve, demand is elastic at high prices, unit elastic at the midpoint, and inelastic at low prices — so TR is maximized at the midpoint.

Worked example

A gym charges $40/month and has 1,000 members. It cuts the price to $30 and membership rises to 1,400. Using the midpoint method: %ΔQ = 400/1,200 = 33.3%; %ΔP = -10/35 = -28.6%. Ed = 33.3 / -28.6 = -1.17, so |Ed| = 1.17 > 1 — demand is elastic. Check revenue: old TR = 40 x 1,000 = $40,000; new TR = 30 x 1,400 = $42,000. Revenue rose, confirming the elastic-demand rule.

Income and cross elasticities

Income elasticity Ei = %ΔQ / %ΔI: Ei > 0 = normal good; Ei > 1 = luxury (income-elastic); 0 < Ei < 1 = necessity; Ei < 0 = inferior good. Cross-price elasticity Exy = %ΔQx / %ΔPy: positive ⇒ substitutes (Coke and Pepsi); negative ⇒ complements (printers and ink); near zero ⇒ unrelated.

Determinants of price elasticity

  • Substitutes: more and closer substitutes ⇒ more elastic.
  • Necessity vs luxury: necessities are inelastic.
  • Budget share: big-ticket items are more elastic.
  • Time: demand is more elastic in the long run as buyers adjust.

How to apply it

Estimate elasticity (from historical data or experiments), then set price toward the elastic range only if the goal is volume, or exploit inelastic segments for margin. Elasticity also feeds the markup pricing rule used in Lesson 7 and the marginal-revenue logic of Lessons 1 and 6.
full_html:
Demand and elasticity form the empirical and analytical backbone of pricing, forecasting, and revenue management. This lesson develops the demand function, the family of elasticity measures, their relationship to revenue, their determinants, and the estimation issues a manager confronts in practice.

From the law of demand to the demand function

The law of demand — an inverse relation between price and quantity demanded, holding other factors constant — follows from the substitution and income effects formalized by Slutsky (1915) and Hicks and Allen (1934), explored fully in Lesson 3 (Consumer Theory). The market demand function generalizes to Qd = f(P, I, Ps, Pc, T, E, N) where Ps and Pc are prices of substitutes and complements, T tastes, E expectations, and N the number of buyers. A movement along the curve (Δ quantity demanded) arises only from a change in own price; a change in any other argument shifts the curve (Δ demand). Marshall (1890) crystallized this apparatus and introduced the elasticity concept itself.

Price elasticity: point and arc

Marshall (1890) defined the price elasticity of demand as Ed = (dQ/dP)(P/Q), a unit-free ratio of proportional changes that permits comparison across goods measured in different units. For discrete changes the arc (midpoint) elasticity Ed = (ΔQ / Q̄) / (ΔP / P̄), using mean base values, removes the direction-dependence of the simple percentage method. A subtlety often missed: elasticity is not the same as slope. A linear demand curve Q = a - bP has constant slope (-b) but elasticity varies continuously along it, from minus infinity at the price intercept to zero at the quantity intercept, passing through -1 at the midpoint.

Elasticity, marginal revenue, and total revenue

The deepest result links elasticity to marginal revenue: MR = P(1 + 1/Ed) (equivalently MR = P(1 - 1/|Ed|)). Several consequences follow. (i) MR is positive only where demand is elastic (|Ed| > 1); thus a profit-maximizing firm with market power (MR = MC > 0) always operates on the elastic portion of demand — never the inelastic portion, where cutting output would raise revenue and lower cost simultaneously. (ii) Total revenue is maximized where MR = 0, i.e., |Ed| = 1. (iii) Rearranging gives the inverse-elasticity (Lerner) pricing rule developed in Lessons 6-7: (P - MC)/P = 1/|Ed|, so the optimal markup is larger the more inelastic the demand. This is the bridge from demand estimation to pricing strategy.

Income elasticity and good classification

Income elasticity Ei = (%ΔQ)/(%ΔI) classifies goods: Ei < 0 inferior (e.g., bus travel, instant noodles); 0 < Ei < 1 normal necessity (income-inelastic, e.g., food staples); Ei > 1 luxury (income-elastic, e.g., business-class travel). Engel's law (Engel, 1857) — that the budget share spent on food falls as income rises — is the historical empirical anchor here. Income elasticities are central to demand forecasting: a recession hurts luxury sellers far more than necessity sellers, a fact managers must build into scenario plans.

Cross-price elasticity

Cross-price elasticity Exy = (%ΔQx)/(%ΔPy) measures relatedness: Exy > 0 ⇒ substitutes (when Pepsi's price rises, Coke's quantity rises); Exy < 0 ⇒ complements (when printer prices fall, ink sales rise); Exy ≈ 0 ⇒ independent goods. This statistic underpins antitrust market definition (the SSNIP test) and competitive strategy, since it quantifies how exposed a firm is to rivals' pricing.

Worked example

A streaming service raises price from $10 to $12 (a 20% increase via the simple method, or %ΔP = 2/11 = 18.2% via midpoint). Subscribers fall from 2.0m to 1.85m (midpoint %ΔQ = -0.15/1.925 = -7.8%). Arc elasticity = -7.8% / 18.2% = -0.43, so |Ed| = 0.43 — inelastic. Revenue rises from $20.0m to $22.2m, consistent with the inelastic rule. But suppose a new rival launches; cross-price effects raise the firm's elasticity to, say, 1.6. Now the same price rise would shrink revenue, showing how competitive entry (Lesson 6) and substitute availability dynamically change the elasticity the manager faces.

Determinants of elasticity

  • Availability and closeness of substitutes — the dominant driver; narrowly defined goods (a specific brand) are far more elastic than broad categories (all soft drinks).
  • Necessity vs luxury and degree of "must-have."
  • Budget share — salt is inelastic partly because it is trivial in the budget.
  • Time horizon — short-run demand for gasoline is inelastic, long-run far more elastic as buyers switch to efficient cars and transit.
  • Durability and the possibility of postponement.

Estimation and common mistakes

Empirically, demand is estimated by regression, controlled price experiments, or conjoint/discrete-choice models. The central econometric hazard is the identification problem (Working, 1927): observed price-quantity pairs are equilibria of shifting supply and demand, so a naive regression of Q on P confounds the two. Instrumental variables (supply shifters such as input costs) are needed to recover the demand curve. Other frequent errors: (1) confusing a shift in demand with a movement along it; (2) equating slope with elasticity; (3) assuming a single elasticity holds across the whole curve or all customer segments; (4) ignoring the long-run/short-run distinction; and (5) treating elasticity as fixed when entry, innovation, or income shocks change it.

Connections across the syllabus

Elasticity is the connective tissue of the course. It rests on the substitution/income decomposition of Consumer Theory (Lesson 3); through MR = P(1 + 1/Ed) it powers the output rule of Market Structures (Lesson 6); through the inverse-elasticity rule it determines optimal markups and Price Discrimination across segments with different elasticities (Lesson 7); and through income and cross elasticities it drives demand forecasting. A manager who can estimate and interpret elasticities holds the single most actionable number in microeconomics.
